column12:
The crack occurred on the over-mold plastic, and the raw material is PBT 1215, this material is weak for impact resistance.

Material Performance: Elongation at break: {color:#bf2600}2.8{color}, flexural modulus {color:#bf2600}4622{color}, impact resistance: {color:#bf2600}6{color}.
column13:
Change the raw material from PBT1215 to PBT B220，to improve the strength.

Material Performance: Elongation at break: {color:#bf2600}3.5,{color} flexural modulus {color:#ff5630}7550{color}, impact resistance: {color:#ff5630}8{color}.

 CT scan analysis revealed a key assembly defect in the pogo pin configuration:

# *Height Variation* – Inconsistent pogo pin heights observed across the assembly, suggesting non-uniform spring compression, part-to-part variation in pin length, or improper seating depth during assembly.

This height variation is likely causing intermittent or consistent error codes due to poor or inconsistent electrical contact between the pogo pins and their mating contact points.

CT scan imagery confirming height variation across pogo pin array

column12:
Why1.The unit main PCBA malfunction and report NTC open under FCT testing.

Why2.Teardown found anion wire skin broken and connected with NTC terminal J4 which cause NTC open.

Why3.Anion wire skin pitched by CN6 / CN8 / NTC1 pin leg during wiring.

Why4.This is PCBA layout protection design issue, CN6 / CN8 / NTC1 pin leg surface has no yellow glue which is anion wire wiring routine.
column13: 1.Add yellow glue to the CN8 / CN6 / NTC1

column4:
h3. *Issue Description*

We have observed *4 cases* where the *SMD-NTC component was blown*, while the *fuse remained intact and did not trigger*.

----

h3. *Observed Behavior*

* SMD-NTC physically damaged / blown off PCB
* Fuse remains functional 
* Issue observed in multiple units (4 confirmed cases)

----

Initial hypotheses include:

# *Tolerance Stack-Up*
#* Possible combination of resistance tolerance and fuse rating leading to delayed fuse response
# *Component-Related Issues*
#* SMD-NTC lot variation
#* Supplier quality issue
#* Internal defect in SMD-NTC

Zendesk Link: [https://sharkninja.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/22833579|https://sharkninja.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/22833579]
Share point ID: QT20251210-132232
SKU: HD6051S
S/N number: S15ZX512Z0S6 
Time in Field: 1.5 months
Issue: Customer seeing error code (led 1, 4,5,6) with hair straightener attachment
Reproduceable: yes 
Initial Investigation nondestructive testing: 

# Performed resistance measurements between the leads and found that MCH 2 of the straightener not working. No resistance reading possibility of open circuit 
# Performed CT scan and from the Image attached below we can see that one of the wires is cut near the edge. 

Tear down Destructive testing: 
We opened the hair straightener and found that the two MCH wires red and black were damaged. 
black wire was completely cut, and the red wire had the insulation torn. Broken wire is the cause of MCH break.

Zendesk Link: [https://sharkninja.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/23289205|https://sharkninja.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/23289205]
Share point ID: QT20251231-133624
SKU: HD6000PK
S/N number: S04ZX512A1F3 
Time in Field: Out of box 
Issue: Customer seeing error code (led 1, 3) with hair straightener attachment
Reproduceable: yes 
Initial Investigation nondestructive testing: 
1. Performed resistance measurements between the leads and found that NTC 1 of the straightener not working. Resistance of 1.6 K ohm nominal resistance of 3.24-3.25 ohms at room temp (From EE). 
2. Performed CT scan and from the Image attached below we can see that 2 wires from the NTC are close touching each other. 

# Tear down Destructive testing: 
We opened the hair straightener and found that the two NTC wire connections were soldered but not insulated. The wires were touching each other, which likely caused the NTC to not work. This appears to be a manufacturing issue.
column12:
Root Cause：

1,Teflon tube ( used for insulating NTC wire connections where soldered)  was not designed with fixed position, then the tube moved/shifted to other positions during assembly, result in no insulation protection at the NTC copper wire soldering joints, which led to NTC short-circuit and main unit alarm.

 

Escape Cause

1, The function test for main unit with straightener can’t 100% identify this issue, because the two copper wires won’t come into connection all the time although there’s no insulation protection (already done some simulations ) .

2, No inspection requirement for Teflon tube position in straightener assembly line.

3, The MCH sub-assembly line doesn’t conduct a specific enough inspection on the position of Teflon tube , only requires checking whether the tube on the yellow line are loose.

 

System Cause

When changing from heat-shrink tube to Teflon tube didn’t consider or identify the risk of Teflon tube positioning.

Feb/06/2026 12:09 PM;60c0bccf18e9f60071fa23d7;[~accountid:5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309] [~accountid:5b7c1c9f10aacc58d3961949] [~accountid:5d71cc04522f790d9c774905] 

Hi, I’m not comfortable calling the RC verified yet, and I’m concerned the current countermeasure plan (tooling gap fix + wiring geometry change in the PPT) will change two variables at once and we won’t know what may drive improvement. This is important to understand if its a heater specific issue or not.

Can you please add clarity on the following before EB2 signoff:

# Quantify “gaps between outlet inner parts”: What is the measured gap/dim data on the failing unit + additional samples vs drawing/spec and MP baseline? Are parts actually out of spec, or is this tolerance stack / assembly? (Also consider tooling drift by lot/cavity if applicable.)
# Isolate airflow vs heater integration: Provide A/B results (or plan) showing ΔT at the release button for:
#* seal gaps only (temporary external seal)
#* MP-equivalent wiring routing only
#* both combined
# Define wiring geometry difference: Share CT scans with digital measuments of the coil in question from this Jira failure. Review IQC controls and see how it compares to MP baseline, as well as other coils from the same heater and question and others in the quarntined production lot.

Once we have that, we can justify the tooling change + wiring change and understand how much each contributes to reducing touch temps.

The testing team has been working on a protocol for clamping force that has finally been developed: [Clamping Force 1.pptx|https://europro365-my.sharepoint.com/:p:/g/personal/swoo_sharkninja_com/IQCgPXITM2QxSIqOg6FLDihGAVyH31S9ZIssr2fu_SqCaSU?e=xS8FrN&isSPOFile=1&%3D%3D&sdata=NEIrYjRQS25NRU01YUJZTGdXWFh5WWhROUx0Q0hSbnYzdkFYdW42OERSYz0%3D&ovuser=276bce15-bc90-4de5-8559-6c504d8b6cf8%2Chagrawal%40sharkninja.com]

* The test measures the clamping force required to achieve pressure at the center of the plates
* We ran 3 Tiffany units and failed on 2 of those units
* Currently, Tiffany requires significantly more force compared to other straighteners and the pressure is very toe-heavy.
* This aligns with what we’ve seen previously: the toe side of the tress styles better than the heel side.
column12: The team believes that reducing pressure on the toe side should help bring the pressure toward the center while also lowering the overall clamping-force requirement.

0.5mm assembly difference is very hard to control and after assembly the MCH will has some movement - this is we considered as assembly tolerance

column18:
Dec/16/2025 4:39 PM;712020:8b518423-012e-422c-8345-f7190602a9c5;[~accountid:712020:335537e7-4283-4cfa-a78f-973b0cd63d60] [~accountid:634cd263d66d8108a1210009] [~accountid:624f8ec9f6a2690069620ea4] [~accountid:5d5e8f18349a680d05e6852e] [~accountid:5d7914ad8ce6b60c3810e5df] 
I am unclear what the failure mechanism being proposed here is?
Is the suggestion that when the product is dropped that the MCH slides on the thermal paste and the gap called out in the images is closed?
Is the suggestion the MCH moves in the direction shown in the image below?:

!Screenshot_20251216_213105_Firefox.jpg|width=1460,height=700,alt="Screenshot_20251216_213105_Firefox.jpg"!


Given the MCH has little mass and the thermal paste holds it I am not confident that the MCH can impact the end wall of the MCH top cap with sufficent force to break it.

I would expect that the failure is due to the plates being compressed together hard and the MCH being forced into the surface of the top cap shown in the image below.
To break the ceramic the force needs to be unevenly applied to the MCH.
I would suggest that excessive thermal paste could make the MCH sit closer to the top cap ceiling. How well controlled is the application of thermal paste? Could it be uneven and too thick?
Also is how well toleranced is the covering that the MCH wires terminate in to? Could this be taller on the MCH that failed? 
Can the failed MCH be measured or has it been destroyed / lost?

!Screenshot_20251216_213418_Firefox.jpg|width=1776,height=740,alt="Screenshot_20251216_213418_Firefox.jpg"!

In the image below there is a small recess below the MCH terminal.
Can this be made larger to ensure there is clearance between the Top cap and the MCH terminal

!Screenshot_20251216_213554_Firefox.jpg|width=1458,height=598,alt="Screenshot_20251216_213554_Firefox.jpg"!
column19:

Can you measure some heater assemblies to review how thickly the thermal paste is applied - Is the MCH heater height consistent across heater assembly(blue arrow dimension)
 Is the wire terminal (green arrow dimension) consistent across MCH heaters

!20251217_155333.jpg|width=3000,height=4000,alt="20251217_155333.jpg"!
column20:
Dec/19/2025 1:28 AM;634cd263d66d8108a1210009;Hi [~accountid:712020:8b518423-012e-422c-8345-f7190602a9c5] [~accountid:712020:335537e7-4283-4cfa-a78f-973b0cd63d60] We measured several the current MB samples and found the below gap had more than 1.5mm.

!image-20251219-071537.png|width=978,height=472,alt="image-20251219-071537.png"!

From EB to MB samples all the ARB test and Drop test and other test, we didn’t find the MCH broken issue, only one pc found on EB3.5 ARB.

This is individual case and very low risk for MP.

We tested the remain unit from EB/EB2/EB3/EB3.5 each 10 pcs for different direction to simulate the drop test and didn’t find this MCH broken issue.

we also measured the MCH compressive resistance for HD600 Gen2 and HT400 MCH, form the data the HT400 MCH is stronger than HD600 Gen2. we didn’t receive any complaints for consumer on HD600 Gen2 about this issue, so we should have fully confidence on HT400. 

!image-20251219-074346.png|width=304,height=414,alt="image-20251219-074346.png"!

we want to let the MCH far away the MCH Top cover during assemble process and reduce risk of the MCH touch the edge of cover as the interim action.

From the broken location on the solder slot, we want to make below changes to reduce risk of the MCH broken as the long team action.

* Reduce the MCH of slot width from 2.94mm to 2.16mm.
* Reduce the MCH of slot distance to edge from 3.49mm to 4.14.
* Add the radius on the side of slot
* Above of action can strength the MCH and reduce broken risk.

!image-20251219-080013.png|width=1507,height=378,alt="image-20251219-080013.png"!

As this is individual case and very low risk for MP, suggest move OPT and improve it post-MP by ECN.

This has not been seen on any other HT400 builds.
Reviewing with core the only other similar issue was found on HD600 Active Round Brush 
[https://sharkninja.atlassian.net/browse/VALENTINO-864|https://sharkninja.atlassian.net/browse/VALENTINO-864|smart-link]
column26:
Dec/24/2025 6:22 PM;712020:4fe183f4-ec18-49a3-a26f-f933f570a361;[~accountid:712020:8b518423-012e-422c-8345-f7190602a9c5] I’ve had a look at chatted with Shannon and Harshita.

With n=1 it’s hard to know if this is a one-off or a broader concern. I’d like to look at the sample to understand the mechanism of failure: do we have evidence that this is impact, thermal, or pre-damage from assembly? 

Questions:

* do we know the use history of this sample, how the failure induced or occurred?
* Do we have the capability to do detailed failure analysis - fracture surface microscopy etc to determine how this crack initiated? If not can we engage an SME?



If we understand the mechanism of failure then we can devise a proper test case to validate the corrective action.

Thanks, and Happy Christmas!

* Unit is from internal SharkNinja Employee
* Teardown revealed hair ingress + dark spots on receptacle prongs + seemingly chipped power cord metal
* Unit was cleaned from hair ingress, black spots on receptacle unable to be removed
* Unit re-assembled, during test, INT power ocurred
* Microscope inspection showed chips on power cord were small divots in metal.
* EE reviewed images and mentioned potential arcing in connection, which causes these divots/chips in the metal
* Corresponding black spots on receptacle prongs also seem to be chipped/divots, but hard to tell.

Static arm on some units are experiencing low temps. We have tested with and without airflow


Ntc temps look good - plates are low

!image-20250912-183541.png|width=457,height=312,alt="image-20250912-183541.png"!

!image-20250912-183514.png|width=480,height=270,alt="image-20250912-183514.png"!


On several P4 and EB1 units, the static arm is recovering to a lower temperature than the moving arm when there is airflow. When airflow is turned off, it seems like the plate temps match.

[+Baseline EB1s.pptx+|https://europro365.sharepoint.com/:p:/s/2025SharkRoadMapInnovation/EYua3SnQh-NNvzvNozugH2YBuc8LNTBDVJ5URWrzZVpF2Q?e=uUgmbA]

!image-20250911-142648.png|width=1600,height=900,alt="image-20250911-142648.png"!
column12:
We found on EB1 Unit #83 that the plate backer was in physical contact with the duct. Before/insulating showed that insulating the plate from the duct brought the plate temps back up to match the static arm.



!image-20250912-183956.png|width=4032,height=3024,alt="image-20250912-183956.png"!



!image-20250912-183941.png|width=3024,height=4032,alt="image-20250912-183941.png"!
column13:
WE need to look at the clearance spacing between the duct and the static arm plate assembly. it seems like the clearance tolerance currently in the design is not large enough to leave a gap between the plate backer and the duct.

1. design in more clearance (tooling implications). 2. we could insulate the plate assembly (cost) 3. we could use a larger bumper to stand off the plate from the duct (seemingly small impact)

column19:
Oct/20/2025 9:37 PM;61b4a597fe9f3000683df097;[~accountid:712020:32dda18a-7760-4836-abb9-c429996adc58] [~accountid:5cdd7edb25d61c0dc61526e2] 

CC [~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80] [~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] 

I have attached the DQTP protocol and key screenshots.

The voltage drop to 0V lasts only 0.5 cycle, which is *10ms*. and then recovered. 

You know that plugs have many electronic components. Capacitors and coils will provide a brief power supply within *1-25ms* after the power is cut off.

Due to the power outage lasting only 10ms, some units tripped within 10 milliseconds, while others restored voltage before tripping.

All these meet the design requirements of PRCD. The current design can only meet the DQTP Acceptance Criterion C standard.

We will not immediately update the AS and upload it after each SW update, because SW function updates are frequent, and but the PLM system has a phased control over the AS, for example, each EB is only updated once version.

column12:
the inner rib area is rough and there is easily tooling sticking issue during injection.

The stick tension will pull the the surface deformation.
column13:
Tooling modification 

1.The areas of the mold with ribs are polished to reduce the rib pull force during demolding, 

2. the areas outside the ribs are re-textured to increase the roughness, 

thereby enhancing the pulling force and preventing the release failure and adhesion of the mold before demolding, in order to reduce the demolding pull force at the rib positions.

!image-20250827-090325.png|width=465,height=157,alt="image-20250827-090325.png"!

highlight this issue and ensure the QA team can find the issue in MP.

Jul/24/2025 8:42 PM;5d5e8f18349a680d05e6852e;[~accountid:712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5] Checked with supplier yesterday and share some information for your reference

# Pic 1, some area loss of painting, the reason is painting material accumulate at fixtures edge position, this causes component some area was covered then painting material cannot spray onto component. Actions: 1) clean accumulate material from fixtures 2) change to new fixture if accumulate material cannot be cleaned clearly. These actions already implemented from yesterday
# Pic 2 and pic from William, this position was not poor painting but caused by parting line. At initial design for this component was non-painting part and parting line was not so obvious. After adding painting there was no design changing and tooling optimization so painting material very easily accumulate at parting line position. Supplier can do some optimization during painting, but they cannot 100% guarantee no accumulate material during painting because of parting line. They suggest a tooling optimization at this position, [~accountid:634cd263d66d8108a1210009] [~accountid:624f8ec9f6a2690069620ea4] please help to review with tooling team and supplier for tooling modification or optimization available or not, Thanks

column4:
Current Carbon Fiber combs makes a lot of sound going through the hair, especially in dry mode (without air)

We have not seen a sample of our comb, so will need to be validated.  

During consumer testing, we saw this as more of an issue with:

* *Thick and High-Density Hair:* More/Thicker strands = more friction against the comb.
* *Dry or Damaged Hair:* Rougher and split ends can create more sound due to increased friction and resistance
* *Larger Sections:* more strands rubbing against the comb and each other. Takes more force to get through a big section which can lead to more dragging

A normal chasing comb with a straightener typically doesn’t make much noise, but with the comb embedded: 

* The housing of Tiffany is acting like a *soundbox*, amplifying the noise 
* The plates clamp and pull the hair taut, *increasing friction* between the hair and the comb teeth, which produces more resistance and therefore sound. 

Videos: 

!IMG_4403.MOV|width=2160,height=3840,alt="IMG_4403.MOV"!

Dry Ends 

!IMG_4398.MOV|width=2160,height=3840,alt="IMG_4398.MOV"!

Thick Strands and Large Section
column12: The teeth of the comb are very sharp. This sharpness increases friction when the comb is used on hair, which in turn generates more noise. The sharp teeth likely contribute to both the noise issue and potential discomfort or damage to hair during use.

Current testing has been of a design similar to C.
This has shown 

* *Tension:* 3 testers felt the current comb (sharper edges) had more tension; 2 of them were not satisfied, describing it as slightly rough or draggy, especially on finer hair.
* *Glide:* 2 testers said the smoother comb moved slightly easier through the hair, particularly on tangled sections.
* *Sound:* Results were inconclusive, as both 3D-printed versions sounded more “chalky.” We’ll need to revalidate sound once we have tooled parts.

column12:
The parts on this ABR samples is specially supplied by team after MB to verify the hot foil with additional UV coating on the trim and Shark logo location to check if the UV has benefit to improve the peel off. the possible root cause should be from process.

* the resin PC material stayed in the Injection molding cylinder longer once the machine stop in the midway.
* Based on the experience, suspected  with unstable injection process during the initial injection, the the PC material in the part will be in higher or lower temperature, its performance will not be normal and easily crack.

Remark: PC material has high requirement for the injection parameter/process. According to the injection molding requirements, the first 30pcs  shots samples need to be scrapped because at this time, the material has poor plasticization issue, high melt viscosity, poor fluidity,  and the molecular chains have not fully expanded, will  cause to the big  internal stress, low strength and easy brittleness. the next shot samples after initial 30pcs will be very stable and this issue will not happen.- we have never this issue in EB0/EB1/MB other ABR samples.

column4:
Fuse is tested using a multimeter. In some cases, the fuse is destroyed and physically blown up. In other cases, it appears fine but still has no connectivity.

!image-20250620-152058.png|width=276,height=368,alt="image-20250620-152058.png"!
column12:
Why1：Because the fuse F1 and the bridge stack DB1 on the PCBA were burned out.

Why2：Because there is a large current generated in the circuit.

Why3：The positive and negative electrodes in capacitor are separated by electrolytic paper. When the electrolytic paper is damaged, the positive and negative electrodes get touched, forming a large current.

Why4：Because the electrolytic paper was punctured when dropped.
column13:
# Increases the  thickness of the negative electrode pad paper from 30±5μm to 40±5μm to improve the negative electrode pad paper strength.
# Take lesson learnt

EB1:

EB1 oil was from KP which is the supplier of painting supplier YAJOY, which adhesive force will be lower on the glossy surface during the ABR  especially the worker will spray solution on the surface.

MB: in original the ink the curing agent  DB is not enough to pass ABR severe test.
column13:

YAJOY changed the oil supplier to JG, which was used in HD600MP ST2 and have good performance on gloss painting surface. remark: MB  has some improvement, but still has minor wear after ABR, considering ABR is severe test and the result is acceptable. will work with the pad print supplier to explore  further action post-MP.

After MB: improved the ink with curing agent to BS from DB, BS curing agent has a stronger adhesion. updated SOP and ink Curing agent.

column4:
HD600US/INTL mini refresh_MB1 imprived_Unit Check Out(Protocol number: HC-GP-IS-012)_
The airflow indicator light has a delay (in dry mode: when the airflow is adjusted from level three to no wind mode).
column12: Before the unit transitions from high temperature to dry burn, it needs to delay the motor shutdown, to prevent the heater from overheating and burning out.
column13:
Normal function, no FW issue

Need PD confirm with US EE to update the UI Spec.
column14: Jun/02/2025 9:14 PM;59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b;Hi team, here is a normal function, before the unit transitions from high temperature to dry burn, it needs to delay the motor shutdown for approximately 3 seconds. Otherwise, there is a risk that the main heating wire could burn out.
column15: Jun/02/2025 10:22 PM;5bd81045bd2c4c2de48b76b3;[~accountid:712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5] Please review comments from Alan. It meet design intention. Suggest move to close.
column16: Jun/03/2025 8:42 AM;712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5;[~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b] Can you help me understand this more. I haven't noticed this before on my units. Does this only happen when the unit is already hot?
column17: Jun/03/2025 8:46 AM;712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5;[~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b] I have 1 unit with the new FW at Leshow and it doesnt have this long of a delay.
column18:
Jun/03/2025 7:34 PM;712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5;Spoke with Genti about this issue. This is not related to the new FW version for memory. This also happens on EB2 of Gen 2, the LEDS have a faster response but the motor cutout has a delay. Through testing out a few different settings, this only happens on high heat in dry mode. 

Moving to OPT board to optimize the delay response in the LEDs. [~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b] [~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80]  Can you update the corrective action plan?
column19: Jun/11/2025 2:00 AM;634cd263d66d8108a1210009;[~accountid:712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5] Normal function, no FW issue .Need you confirm with US EE to acceptance this or update the UI Spec.
column20: Jun/11/2025 3:50 PM;712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5;[~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b] [~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80] Can you share the corrective action plan for this to optimize the delay response in the LEDs when airflow is turned off?
column21: Jun/20/2025 2:28 AM;5bd81045bd2c4c2de48b76b3;[~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80] Do we have plan to make change for the indicator light delay?
column22:
Jun/20/2025 9:12 AM;62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80;[~accountid:5bd81045bd2c4c2de48b76b3] the team is preparing for this update including the new UI flow update - Active Accessory memory change and UI LED resistance change.

Please meet with [~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b]  and the rest of the CN EE team to put a plan in place for all updates.

Note: we will combine all the active EE changes in one ENC in order to minimize all the EE Approval Sheets updates across all PCBA vendors, which support multiple OEMs across CN and VN. These changes will impact multiple PCBAs, thus there will be a lot of work needed to roll-in the changes via an ECN.

column26:
May/26/2025 2:27 AM;5be0519a741c7e2e7e9dae7b;Hi [~accountid:712020:c607b510-7764-456f-b116-a4b54d28aba5] , [~accountid:712020:e2098dba-25d9-4716-8869-349da59bc305] , just double checked with SN lab for ABR test process.

During ABR test, need spray Coconut Oil and shampoo. 

Wet mode

* Spray 10g water on the hair to make it wet
* Weigh 1g Coconut Oil, then thawing it by hands
* Smear Coconut Oil on whole hair

Dry mode

* Add heat protectant for 0.8g and dry shampoo 0.24g before testing, spray 4 time or 1s

so, I am assuming these Coconut Oil and shampoo have a little chance affect the hair stuck.

column12:
In SZ lab：The current design tests that there will be slight fluctuations in the number of cycles during the dry time, approximately between 20 and 22c/s, which is the current design level



Remark：

A sample will be tested repeatedly three times

If the sample all 20 cycles (each cycle/35s) in three tests, it can meet the 677s specification

677s=(20c/s*35s+20c/s*35s+20c/s*35s)/3

735s=(21c/s*35s+21c/s*35s+21c/s*35s)/3

777s=(22c/s*35s+22c/s*35s+22c/s*35s)/3

column12:
HD600EU plug can satisfy the PQT test (test level in 40%) and fail in test level in 0%, (in 0%, it can be accepted) 

The difference between UK plug and EU plug is the Spring specification, the “Reset“key is controlled by electromagnetic force and spring force, when voltage dips to 40%, the electromagnetic force small than the spring force, the “Reset“key  disconnected

column4:
*Summary:*
Unit returned with no call data on file. Ingram flagged it as no power. After basic checks and teardown, failure appears related to a thermal event at the NTC, possibly caused by incomplete glue coverage.



*Findings:*

* No CX data or reported issue from the user.
* Ingram confirmed unit was completely non-functional (no power).
* SN team tested the fuse on the board — it was intact, and current moved through properly.
* Power cord tested separately — no issues found.
* Opened the unit and found visible damage to the NTC.
* Evidence of a thermal event near the NTC; signs point to incomplete glue coverage around nearby wires that may have caused overheating or a short.

column4:
Unit returned with signs of electrical damage. Teardown revealed the following:

* Large quantity of hair observed in the swirl contact socket
* Arcing/discharge path visible on the largest ring of the male contact
* Black discoloration present in swirl contact socket (evidence of thermal or electrical damage)
* NTC component is intact
* 4A fuse was blown, indicating that the protection mechanism was activated
* Burn mark observed at the top-right corner of Q2, likely due to localized overheating or shorting

column4:
Temperature inconsistencies were observed during TC probe testing at the *unit outlet (EB0.7 #14)*, across different *RPM settings* controlled by the BLDC fan. Although the BLDC changes RPM to vary airflow, the final outlet air temperature is expected to remain consistent. 

However, both *maximum* and *average* outlet temperatures *decrease as RPM is reduced*, indicating a potential issue where airflow rate is unexpectedly influencing thermal output.

!image-20250404-121915.png|width=380,height=193,alt="image-20250404-121915.png"!
column12: The output air temperature is affected by the air flow speed, and is not linear.
column13:
The software incorporates a nonlinear air temperature micro-adjustment.

-The LFHH Dry mode software compensates the 7C target temperature

column29:
Jun/12/2025 9:03 AM;712020:5af55cc4-667b-4271-82e5-ea7dc8ba0809;[~accountid:712020:32dda18a-7760-4836-abb9-c429996adc58] may show a higher delta due to the unit itself, but it doesn't reflect the performance of all UK units. This test was important to observe the overall trend. With the new firmware, the average temperature delta dropped from 16.5°C to 6°C — a significant improvement. For comparison, the US and EU units showed an average temp difference of around 3°C with the same firmware, and all units met the KPI. This confirms that the new firmware is effective.

If we still believe this is a concern, I recommend testing additional US, EU, and UK units (3 units from each SKU). If the results show that UK units consistently have a higher temperature delta, we should consider updating the firmware with new compensation coefficients specifically for the UK units, but I don’t think we have time for it anymore.

Jul/02/2025 4:18 PM;712020:5af55cc4-667b-4271-82e5-ea7dc8ba0809;Hello [~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] , [~accountid:5b523c3a628cd32ca1bee0ea] , [~accountid:712020:32dda18a-7760-4836-abb9-c429996adc58]  and team,
I ran the TC probe test on the MB units in both *Dry* and *Wet* modes. In both modes, the temperature across different airflow settings was inconsistent. Notably, in Dry mode, the delta between the min outlet avg. temp and max outlet avg. temp was smaller.

It seems that hotter tines may help reduce this delta, suggesting that the compensation coefficients might need to vary not only by airflow mode but also by heat mode.

column4:
HD432CR fuse was blown. No current running though. 

!Image (61).jfif|width=768,height=1024,alt="Image (61).jfif"!
column12:
Why1：Because the fuse F1 and the bridge stack DB1 on the PCBA were burned out.

Why2：Because there is a large current generated in the circuit.

Why3：The positive and negative electrodes in capacitor are separated by electrolytic paper. When the electrolytic paper is damaged, the positive and negative electrodes get touched, forming a large current.

Why4：Because the electrolytic paper was punctured when dropped.
column13:
# Increases the  thickness of the negative electrode pad paper from 30±5μm to 40±5μm to improve the negative electrode pad paper strength.
# Take lesson learnt

column29:
Apr/24/2025 5:11 AM;634cd263d66d8108a1210009;We are still waiting for MB result.  [~accountid:625dbcb8ab7a1800708a6a17] Please notify me firstly once the test is completed.

But we retest the EB0 samples and checked all the related parts size and FAI they were all meet 2D drawings Spec. 

As the Polisher has more curved and smaller outlets, with high air pressure it is easy to cause return air. the heat air follows the assemble gap to leak and cause the surface high temp.  As below schematic diagram, this is why the surface high temp.

!image-20250424-065800.png|width=681,height=422,alt="image-20250424-065800.png"!

we made improved samples with adding 0.08mm in inner connector surface to reduce the gap. the surface temp had a big reduction. This assembly gap is the most important cause of temperature increase. But we have a difficult removal force and have a theory tolerance interference. so, we didn’t recommend reducing the gap again because all gaps meet the tolerance analysis and All accessories are shared.

!image-20250424-075828.png|width=270,height=294,alt="image-20250424-075828.png"!

Comparison with other SKU and have the same issue and the Spec as below.

!image-20250424-075606.png|width=880,height=140,alt="image-20250424-075606.png"!

So we suggest changing the Spec:  Primary Surfaces: ≤75 °C / Secondary Surfaces: ≤95 °C and passed the UL test low risk to close this ticket.

column12: Design issue: The ELE Gen3 ALCI shares the similar design as MP used ELE Gen2 ALCI, which is designed based on UL943B. It’s a standard/common part for all customers including SN. The EFT, ESD, LVT are all SN internal testing requirements, where the ELE claimed may can’t pass by chance.
column13: The same level as MP, accept as normal. will running change to 2IN1 special model for SN which will meet SN testing requirements
column14: Feb/12/2025 5:37 AM;5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309;[~accountid:615c5172d9820f0070af4d12]  The EFT test is SN internal testing, either the existing MP ELE ALCI or the Gen3 in the ECN process is a common model not specifically design for SN. they can’t pass the SN internal EFT testing.  BTW, The failure model is acceptable which has no functional failure, the testing unit just shut down but can still restore normal by pressing the RESET button. suggest to close this ticket.
column15: Feb/12/2025 7:59 AM;615c5172d9820f0070af4d12;[~accountid:5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309] did we see this failure with current MP plug? Can I look at that Jira ticket.
column16:
Feb/12/2025 8:45 AM;6124bfff0952180069d65b9d;[~accountid:5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309] I would hesitate to close this ticket just because it is an internal test. We have the test for a reason so I’d like to fully understand why the unit fails - the root cause listed doesn’t explain the issue.

My understanding is that there are three main reasons for an EFT test:

# *Operational Stability:* Ensure the plug does not trip unexpectedly due to noise
# *Protection Functionality:* Verify that the ALCI still correctly detects ground faults and trips when necessary after exposure to electrical spikes
# *Compliance with Standards:* Ensures the ALCI meets compliance - appreciate that this test is an internal test. Please confirm passing compliance and share test information

With this in mind, I would like to see the following before we consider closing this ticket:

* Clear root cause
* Risk of not passing this test - what are the product safety/reliability risks?
* POV on corrective action or why you think it is acceptable to proceed and not pass the test
column17:

the next step is running change to special designed 2IN1 ALCI plug for SN to be able to pass these internal tests.

[~accountid:712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4] [~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80] I’d be interested in your POV on these failures too please
column23:
Feb/26/2025 8:57 PM;5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309;[~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d]  to explain more

# LVT and EFT test is a simulation of noise/shock from electric network. If such noise happens in electric network as high as below Pic shown, it may lead to the unit shut down when in use. Customer needs to press the “Reset” button on the ALCI plug to restore the power. There’s no issue for the function/safety to the unit
# The pass/fail criteria of SN LVT and EFT is that “ the unit should be able to automatically restore after the shock, instead of customer to manually intervene.
# Current MP ALCI plug has no such module to deal with, but we’re running change to 2IN1 ALCI plug which is specially designed to deal with such issues. 
# From test results. If the EFT failed, the only impact is customer need to press the “RESET” button to restore the power. no harm to the unit itself, nor the user.

Mar/21/2025 12:38 AM;62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80;[~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] I’ve added you to the Teams' conversation regarding the cross functional team discussion/agreement on this ticket. 

Again, the summary is:

These live voltage faulty conditions (EFT, LVT, ESD) are rare in homes, they are caused by service provider equipment issues - transformer at the poll issues, or lightning strikes during storm days, or they can be caused by hard switching/loose plugs during operations. 

 *Therefore, as long as Gen3 ALCI plug is not getting damaged or the unit is not getting damaged then EE agrees with approving this ECN.* 

 In addition, i'd be supportive to have ELE provide data between Gen3 and Gen2 in order to prove that Gen3 has the same/similar performance to Gen2. The measurements need to be done on the same main unit.
column37:
Mar/21/2025 2:59 PM;60c0bccf18e9f60071fa23d7;[~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80]  [~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d]  [~accountid:5fc98f7747ac91006f0be309] [~accountid:712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4] [~accountid:712020:ad077baf-8712-4c15-acb9-0f6dc247cd1e] 

I have observed that more times than not, customers call reporting no power, and in most cases, the issue is recoverable by resetting the ALCI or cleaning their filter. This occurs often enough that our agents are trained to check the plug and walk the customer through resetting it.

From my review of call center data, these EFT-induced trips align with real-world conditions, where transient voltage events can occur due to:

* Hard Switching & Loose Connections – Customer plugging/unplugging or using poor-quality outlets.
* Household Electrical Noise – Large appliances like HVAC systems, refrigerators, and microwaves generating voltage transients
* Utility Grid Disturbances – Power fluctuations from transformer switching, storms, or voltage spikes

Since the Gen3 ALCI does not get damaged and the unit itself recovers fully after RESET, this is more of a customer experience issue rather than a safety or reliability concern.

I agree with approving the ECN based on prior MP acceptance of similar cases and minimal impact to the CX.  I also support ELE providing comparative performance data between Gen3 and Gen2 ALCI on the same units to validate no functional regression.

One of the attachments- turbo concentrator won't connect.​

HD700 concentrators wont click into place and will fall out. On the same unit, a HD500 concentrator will work.

Noise is a key driver for reviews 4 stars and below. Our product noise spec was increased form 83 dBA to 87dBA.​

* Noise results from the SZ lab coming in around 83-84.5 dBA​



Some Customer reviews:

_-Only drawback is the noise._ *_It is very loud. I actually wear earplugs when using it._*

-I waited about 6 months to review this so I could get a good handle on it. *It loses 1 star because it is LOUD.* I use earbuds when I'm drying my hair because the high pitched noise hurts.

-I don’t know what I was expecting *but it is like having a loud vacuum beside your ears for the duration of styling your hair.* I am returning it due to the noise because it also didn’t dry my hair significantly faster than a quieter dryer and *I can not justify wearing ear protection to dry my hair .*

* *Customer's Concerns:*
** The original unit, purchased via Amazon, was reported to have intermittent power issues and error lights blinking.
* *Agent Actions:*
** Customer was advised to return the original unit in its entirety, including attachments, for the replacement process to proceed.
* *Relevant Notes:*
** Call reason: Customer was initially advised to hand the defective unit directly to FedEx for return but later informed this was incorrect.
** Troubleshooting details from 12/26/2024:
*** Issue involved blinking error lights on the main unit (left airflow and all 3 temp indicators).
*** Troubleshooting was unsuccessful. Return label sent to CX.
* *Additional Findings (1/13/2025):*
** Technical inspection of the defective unit revealed:
*** Pogo pin in the accessory port sticking and not springing back.
*** Black marks observed on the pogo pin.
*** LED indicators (1, 4, 5, 6) were flashing with both the straightener and the fusion brush.

column12:
EB0.7

The test method does not comply with the laboratory test protocol:
There are only three thermocouples; The bottom position will significantly decrease in temperature due to the influence of air flow and it cannot be used as a reference point in the calculation tine temp

EB0.8

Dry mode MHLF and LHLF the tine temperature over the spec.

In different modes, MCH NTC is the same target value. Different Heater airflow will affect different temperature of MCH, thus further affecting the temperature of Tine
column13:
EB0.7

1.Need place 11 thermocouples

!image-20250108-065931.png|width=167,height=245,alt="image-20250108-065931.png"!

!image-20250108-070050.png|width=171,height=128,alt="image-20250108-070050.png"!

2.Run 15min, and calculate the average temperature of 10min-15min for 11 thermocouples

3. The three hottest temperature points are selected to calculate the average value

4.After ambient temperature compensation, the final Tine temp is calculated

!image-20250108-070406.png|width=1374,height=470,alt="image-20250108-070406.png"!

EB0.8

Set different compensation values at different flow mode by software

column26:
Apr/01/2025 11:14 AM;6124bfff0952180069d65b9d;Adding notes from email:

!image-20250401-151355.png|width=1204,height=677,alt="image-20250401-151355.png"!



There is a glaring disparity between tine temperatures (points 4, 5 and 6). [~accountid:712020:5af55cc4-667b-4271-82e5-ea7dc8ba0809] has started some work on mapping these temperatures on EB0.8 but this is an area we would like to improve.

*Some notable issues:*

* TC11 is *very cool* in DRY mode HH/HF mode (169C vs spec of 220C)
** All TCs (except TC2 and TC4 decreased in temperature when flow was turned on (HH/NF vs HH/HF)
*** This would suggest airflow is having a cooling effect on the tines. Root causing underway for this but important next steps and tests:
**** Repeat HH/NF vs HH/HF testing with TCs added to fuse and on back of heater. We will test this in Boston ([~accountid:712020:5af55cc4-667b-4271-82e5-ea7dc8ba0809] ) but [~accountid:61b4a597fe9f3000683df097] could you please also run this testing in Suzhou for a great sample size?
***** Determine if cooling effect is coming from external airflow cooling effect on outer face of tines or if internal airflow is having a cooling effect on heater or in heater cavity (not design intent and could suggest an internal leak)
* In WET mode HH/HF, only TC4 and TC5 are in-spec (120C +/- 5C)
** This is less clear to me why this would be the case. [~accountid:62a7666cddc560006e8a8e80] [~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b]  could we please review the NTC set points for each mode? I would appreciate also if you could share your POV on any FW/EE reasons that this could be occurring

I understand there is some confusion about test method but we should be striving for improved evenness across each tine. Top 3 hottest temps averaged does not feel like a suitable way to ensure evenness. [~accountid:712020:32dda18a-7760-4836-abb9-c429996adc58]  for reference

I have attached a screenshot of Nikita's test data below and added these notes to [HT300-216|https://sharkninja.atlassian.net/browse/HT300-216?atlOrigin=eyJpIjoiYWI5NzJhZGNkYTdkNDQyOTkzZmYwMTRiMDEwOWU4YmYiLCJwIjoiaiJ9]:

column12:
-NTC and MCH wires of heater plate in Front Handle are broken during open/close endurance test.

-the wires can bear so much bending of 30kc/s during test in so impact space.

summarized the test issue, possible root cause as below and need more validation

# LED 1,3 flash-pin sudden poor contact during test
# LED 1,4,5,6 flash-wires through post are broken during test, Fuse temperature is higher than holding temp (Th) spec,then will be broken during test.@

The only reason that the part is fully immersed in fluid is to expedite any areas requiring a more extended test period. That is generally why we only have to run for 168H versus reproducing the worst-case scenario that a user would have, which would require longer periods of dwell between exposure to the chemical. (CC: [~accountid:712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4] [~accountid:615c5172d9820f0070af4d12] ). 

It is very likely that a hair dryer would be exposed to both tap water droplets and Windex droplets regularly while sitting on a countertop. So, it’s important we we continue to try to pass this test and we cannot reduce the standard. 

How is the feasibility test going on EB1.5? Why aren’t we confident that this well help? Have we looked into a second UV coat? Would that help?

Feb/14/2025 12:06 PM;615c5172d9820f0070af4d12;[~accountid:5e254e40832d730ca200134c] PD has aligned to move forward with MP. 

# *Chemical Aging Test Failure*: Based on the test protocol we believe that the bubbling samples are worst case scenario. The test submerges the unit in the solution for 168 hours so that all areas are exposed and represents an accelerated real-world scenario through the life of the unit (enables us to test only to 168 hours). While we believe the protocol is appropriate we have outlined below why we feel that the risk is low.
## This protocol provides a worst case scenario
## The bubbles disappeared after a few hours after the testing
## The likelihood of the consumer placing a unit on a wet surface/counter top for an extended length of time and in the same orientation is unlikely. Potentially a bubble could appear on a specific spot on decal if exposed to enough liquid/solution but we have found that they disappear and we deem this low risk. For your reference the protocol is listed in blue below: 
# *Passing all other DQTP:* We have confidence in the durability and reliability of the decal due to passing DQTP results across all other relevant testing; humidity, scratch-resistance and environmental tests.

column12:
1、Rust issue: The material of motor bearing is Gcr15, Gcr15 must be rusty after environment test, 

2、Water stain issue: Environmental Conditioning；65 degrees@8H, -18degrees@8H,32degrees 85%humidity@8H=1 cycle, total:10 c/s. ，water stain is normal because the machine is not waterproof

so no further action

column12: After the ink is mixed with the curing agent, it is placed for more than 6 hours, resulting in the curing agent reacting with the ink before the screen printing part, and the curing performance is reduced.
column13:
The pad printing workshop increases the ink ratio/use record table, records the start and end time of production, and adds strict requirements in the record sheet that the ink can not be parked for more than 6 hours after the use .

QC first sample table added 20min running machine verification screen printing status requirements.


Update PFEMA to increase the risk identification and prevention measures of poor screen printing caused by too long ink placement time.

We layout the temperature sensor on the ST inner surface, found the temp up to more than 300C, now we can rarely find plastic material to resist so high temp.

!image-20241106-092309.png|width=589,height=358,alt="image-20241106-092309.png"!

column19:
Nov/16/2024 8:03 AM;634cd263d66d8108a1210009;[~accountid:5cbe1c2dcf9b780e6a39fd1e] Pls see below restrict Air test summary with different material in Leshow and YDC. We had planned to switch to PPS to improve restricted air outlet/inlet test.

Restrict Outlet test with PPS YDC EB0 samples is OK, but it’s still failed on Blocked air-inlet test.  

!image-20241119-081438.png|width=1085,height=218,alt="image-20241119-081438.png"!

Spark was caused by exposed wires shortage between upper NCT wire (yellow wire) with lower MCH Neutral wire (white wire) after running only 7minutes. (Standard: run 30 minutes)              {color:#bf2600}(Drape test run 7.5H, maybe confuse this test){color}

!image-20241116-131755.png|width=1136,height=370,alt="image-20241116-131755.png"!

Since the test is run with an open loop FW and NTC doesn’t regulate the air temp, the temp in the air path can go above 300 degrees, PA and PPS material were melt and than wires were exposed to extremely high temp causing the skin to melt. When exposed wires touched each other, it caused the explosion and spark.

As we know, this test is not a compliance test. According to all the test result, unit with active accessories is very difficult to pass this abnormal test. CN team is proposing to cancel this test and back to PA+GF material as the PPS is more brittle and more expensive. Could you share your comments?

column12:
* no cool air slot on lower cover (P0.5)
* leakage caused by bad sealing between collar and main unit 
* air flow come into top cover
* air leakage between rear inner housing and air flue.
column13:
* increase cool air path aera of lower cover.
* reduce the upper pression plate length for better heat dissipation
* add aerogel on upper cover and lower cover
* increase sealing interference between collar(OM) and main body to 0.35mm from 0.2mm
* increase holes on lower cover (P0.5 have no holes)
* update the KPI definition for the primary and secondary contact area.
* after MB -improve the leakage between the rear inner housing and air flue, add the seal rib 0.1mm high for better engagement between the rib and silicone.

column4:
* During a comparison test between the Valentino ARB (MP) and the Burberry product, 5-10 strands of type 2 hair were caught in the tines of the Valentino ARB.
* The incident occurred on Day 2 of hair styling, where the hair had been sprayed wet and products like volumizing mousse and heat protectant were used. This differs from previous trials where no hair snagging was observed without these products.
column12:
* *Thermal Expansion*: Material expansion due to heat may cause the tines to shift, narrowing gaps and increasing the risk of hair snagging.
* *Poor Part Finish*: Rough or uneven surfaces on the tines may catch hair, especially when coated with styling products.
* *Poor Paint Finish*: Inconsistent paint application could increase surface friction, leading to hair snagging.
column13:
* *Widen Upper Tine Gaps*: We will test manually widening the gaps in the upper tine to allow freer movement of hair through the brush, potentially eliminating snagging.
* *Add Stand-offs in the Upper Tine*: To maintain consistent spacing between the tines and avoid gap closure, we will trial adding stand-offs.
* *Smooth the Tine Edges*: Adding a radius to the upper tine aperture to smooth out the hair path and reduce snag points.
* *Die-Cast the Tines in One Piece*: Revisit the option of die-casting the tines in a single piece to avoid assembly-related inconsistencies.
* *Extrude Plates in One Piece*: Explore extruding the plates in a single piece to improve structural integrity, though this will require a cost analysis.
* *Sub-flush Surface Adjustment*: Investigate whether reducing the hair contact surface to be sub-flush with the PPS tine cover will allow hair to bridge over and not snag.

column12:
The Main body and inlet bracket are assembled by hooks, and the current fit is the best that can be done.
This is the structural limitation that the hook interference quantity needs to be balanced for easy assembly with Drop test

If the assembly size had been large, this would have led to assembly difficulties, so based on balanced assembly convenience and Drop test, The team accepted that there is a probability that the hook will come off when the ABR drop.

Remark: This can be restored
column13:

No further good solution

column29:
Nov/07/2024 5:49 AM;59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b;Hi team, the alarm LED4,6 and LED1,4,5,6 is a high probability of appearing in this test. But so far, it can't be reproduced manually. As a result, the root causes are difficult to identify.

# This test is mainly to verify the effect or damage of the ST open-close. Should be a good result. ST is ok.
# Refer to another item test of Dry Run Life Test, the test has been successfully run continuously for 168H. So, simple long-term operation will not affect the software and hardware. We also mimicked the test environment, with 2 separate units+ST running online tests for 12 hours with no warning occurring and sufficient temperature margin on the PCBA. So, SW and HW is ok.
# Back to the actual test, the unit cannot recognize the open-close of the ST, so the unit running logic is the same as that of the dry run life test.
# The possible reason for the alarm is that during the test, the test transmission mechanism and the machine are stressed and deformed, affecting the contact state and causing the alarm. See the below screenshot.
!image-20241107-104544.png|width=908,height=549,alt="image-20241107-104544.png"!
# This test operation and unit force direction will not appear in regular use, so it is extremely low risk, it is recommended to ignore this warning.
# It is recommended to turn off this JIRA.

[~accountid:60c0bccf18e9f60071fa23d7] [~accountid:712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4]  [~accountid:5cdd7edb25d61c0dc61526e2] do you have a POV here?
column31: Nov/11/2024 8:22 PM;59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b;Sorry, Paul, if unit with ST alarms LED4,6 or LED1,3, it can recoverable after power cycling. Once it alarms LED 1,4,5,6, the ST is permanently disabled with thermal fuse, only main unit can recoverable.
column32: Nov/12/2024 10:14 AM;712020:c7ad2e4c-dc8b-44dc-997e-fec147725a95;[~accountid:59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b] 1,4,5&6 is MCH break, what frequency has this error occurred and can you describe the condition?
column33:
Nov/12/2024 11:05 AM;712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4;My POV, this seems like a reliability and nuisance issue depending on why the unit is generating the error and if it will continue to get worse after the first error is thrown on recoverable units. If units are not recoverable. We will see some bricked units that "just died" on the consumer.

This test should not be considered abusive as opening and closing the unit with that frequency seems reasonable.
column34: Nov/14/2024 1:27 AM;5be0519a741c7e2e7e9dae7b;Hi [~accountid:634cd263d66d8108a1210009] , as just reviewed, please share your updates.
column35:
Nov/18/2024 1:34 AM;634cd263d66d8108a1210009;[~accountid:712020:c7ad2e4c-dc8b-44dc-997e-fec147725a95] Total 3pcs to test, 2pcs appeared this MCH fuse broken issue.

!image-20241118-031756.png|width=973,height=341,alt="image-20241118-031756.png"!

*Root cause for MCH fuse broken*: (1,4,5&6 LED flashing).

1.MCH fuse Max temp is 240 but the holding temp (Th) is 200C, if the MCH fuse is in temp of 200C-240C for a long time. the fuse life is only 168h.

2.MCH temperature is 220±10C, Exceed the MCH Fuse Th temperature(200C), Measured the fuse surface temp reach to 210C during the test , so after a run of more than 168hrs, the Fuse easy to break.

!image-20241017-150744.png|width=786,height=608,alt="image-20241017-150744.png"!

!image-20241017-151232.png|width=959,height=261,alt="image-20241017-151232.png"!

Next step: Change new fuse which spec from 240C to 257C (Th change from 200C to 220C) to retest.

Schedule:   

* Find the new fuse to do sample and delivery 11/22
* ST assembly 11/23
* Retest on SZ Lab 11/25-12/2

Aug/28/2024 8:16 AM;712020:365dc051-0990-4922-959f-b153dc851cb4;[~accountid:5d546aa43f5e050c17177d02] , for context, are these the die cast plates? Have we tested the extruded plates? I am curious if the surface of the die-cast is having an effect on the adherence of the coating or if this is a coating issue. Did we run the samples of the plates with bubbling of the coating to the full 168H or did we halt the test? It would be good to see if the bubbling continues on the plates and makes it to a region that has hair contact. 

[~accountid:61131c64a539cb0068f7c5c1] , this is a hair care product that will see wet hair that may still contain oils, hair products, and other items. This is why we use a 5% water solution, as this is the worst case of corrosion over time. From my research, most sweat and other hair products are slightly less salty than this, but they contain salt. Shampoos will adjust their viscosity with the addition of salt. I would be aligned this is only a cosmetic issue up to PD to make the call if the bubbling stays away from the hair contact area of the plates.

column12:
1: Why unit transiently shut off：

   Over-current protection is triggered when swivel is rotating and this protection leads motor stop;

2:  Why over-current protection occurs?

  The PID control in the software requires the speed keeps constant and PWM was increased to keep the intended speed, this increase causes current goes to a high level in a short period:

3: Why PWM was increased in short time?

  A voltage lower than intended number is detected in motor control. 

# Why a voltage lower than intended level is detected?

  When swivel is rotating, grease is applied between swivel and power cord head, and too much grease could cause male and female swivel has disconnection.

# Why voltage drop easily happened on HD600?

  Design change to reduce handle diameter which required to move E-cap out of handle. The distance between E-cap and motor is increased, and system is more sensitive compared with previous projects.
column13:

column32:
Sep/17/2024 8:36 AM;60c0bccf18e9f60071fa23d7;Hi Paul,

I previously conducted an audit on this matter. The mentions I've found from the field seem to indicate that hair is getting caught between the attachment and the main unit, rather than in the outlet ring. Based on this assignment, I was unable to find evidence of hair getting stuck in the outlet ring from field reports.

Here are some relevant customer comments:

* "Hair gets stuck between the brush and the air flow tube."
* "My hair gets caught in the crack where you hook the attachments on."
* "I don’t have to worry about my hair getting stuck and ripping out."
* "My hair gets stuck where the brush connects to the shaft."
* "My hair would get stuck on the rim that locks the brush and the tool itself."
* "My hair constantly got stuck between the brush and the drier."
* "My hair gets caught a lot in the Shark."
* "Your hair can get caught in the grooves where the tool rotates to become a blow dryer."
* "My hair gets caught between the attachment while styling."
* "When using the brush attachments, my hair would get caught between the dryer and the attachment."

We haven't seen many reports from the call center, but there are some TikTok mentions, such as this one:
[https://www.tiktok.com/@kimmeelee/video/7355698498970455297|https://www.tiktok.com/@kimmeelee/video/7355698498970455297|smart-link]

There is still a very small radius on the pin because it must be CNC’d after cutting to meet the surface requirement, there is no way to avoid this radius. column4:
We ran an inlet restriction study with a straightener attached on a flow rate test rig today, and saw “heat NTC  open/short circuit error”.

Main unit was connected on one end of the Venturi tube, and straightener was on the other end. All potential air inlet paths were covered with tape and unit was run on high heat low flow mode. 

# The unit initially would should show “unit inlet full blocked” error within 1 min.
# After 2 runs, it showed “heat NTC  open/short circuit” error
# When I ran the unit again it then showed “MOS over heating” error. 
# I then removed the unit from the test rig, removed some tape, and just ran the unit by itself. It then showed “heat NTC  open/short circuit” again. No heat was coming out from the unit, and the unit was only drawing 21W power. It seemed that the thermal fuse was blown.

column17:
Aug/01/2024 7:58 AM;557058:dd41bfe2-9a5f-413f-b178-46f3dfdae569;[~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] I have gathered some info from the team regarding this failure.

* Explosion was caused by exposed wires shortage. Since the this test is run with an open loop FW, the NTC doesn’t regulate the air temp, so that the air temp can go above 200 degrees. The the air duct split melted and then wires were exposed to extremely high temp causing the skin to melt. When exposed wires touched each other, it caused the explosion.
* Henry already had 2 units with PPS parts tested last week and they both passed the test. However, the air duct split tool wasn’t designed for PPS, and it has very thin feature, so it can’t be used to make a large quantity of PPS parts. A new tool will need to be cut if we want to make this part in PPS for MP. This can be a rolling change. Please also keep in mind that the cost will go up.
* Last but no the least, this is not a compliance test, and the unit is run with an open loop firmware - both NTC and TCO are not working. The CN team is proposing to cancel this test. When they retest this with PPS parts, the main unit fuse was blown due to the high heat.

column20:
Aug/02/2024 5:56 AM;557058:dd41bfe2-9a5f-413f-b178-46f3dfdae569;[~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] 

* I confirmed with[~accountid:5d546aa4acb80e0d853a711c]that this test doesn’t require to have accessories attached.
* Kenny Zhou received feedback from the tooling factory that these tools can’t really be used to make PPS because the steel is too soft. The tool will have scratches after making a few thousand parts.
* Henry had the tooling factory make a few parts in PPS, and repeated this test last week, the MOS blew within 3-4 mins, and the PPS parts didn’t melt at the time. [~accountid:712020:c499734e-c7e1-42fb-ad16-2d022fa8da12] measured the internal temperature in the area shown below, and it reached 300+ degrees. There is a concern that even with PPS parts, it could melt eventually. 
!image-20240802-052138.png|width=41.66666666666667%,alt="image-20240802-052138.png"!
* The current wire sheath material is Teflon, UL1332, which can withstand up to 200 degrees. We are waiting for GB to provide alternative solution. 



In short, since this is not required by UL, and the tool is not feasible to make PPS parts, it should be safe to move forward with this, and then access if we should cut new tools.

Before suggesting solution it would be valuable to double check root cause.

+I believe it could either be caused by:+

# Abnormally high airflow melting material at the inlet of the accessory and making its way into separate cabin when wiring lives. *(do we see a hole at the inlet or opening?)*
## If root cause, material change is reasonable solution.
# OR The build of hot air and pressure of isolated cabin where straightener plates live when run at max temp. *(Is there no pathway of hot airflow from main unit to the material that is melting?)*
## If this is root cause, may need to consider way to relieve the build up of hot air pressure with vents etc.

we can confirm that there are no any holes or big gaps to impact the airflow

Aug/12/2024 3:07 AM;59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b;Hi team, refer to the test data in the following pictures and two videos. The main core reason is:

#  When the PIN surface is soaked with salt water, a layer of salt crystals forms after dried.
# This layer of salt crystals causes a gap between the Pogo Pin and Copper sheet. This gap creates an indeterminate resistance ( depending on the height of the gap). 
# 
!image-20240812-063053.png|width=28.897104128157736%,alt="image-20240812-063053.png"!
a. If this gap resistance causes the resistance between Pin1 and Pin2 to exceed the original value by 5%, the product will alarm LED46.
b. *If this gap resistance causes the resistance between PIN2 and 3 (or PIN2 and PIN4) to exceed ~ 12K, the main unit will likely continue to drive the MCH to heat up to its Fuse broken, and then alarm LED1456.* It should fit this Jira failure.
# c. If this gap resistance causes the resistance between PIN2 and 3 (or PIN2 and PIN4) to exceed ~ 100K, the unit will alarm LED13. 
# d. While this gap resistance causes the resistance between PIN5 and 6 (or PIN5 and PIN7) to exceed ~ 1K(A fuzzy value is difficult to measure and evaluate) , the responsive PIN will spark, and if the spark burned the gap, unit will return to normal work. If not, unit will alarm LED1456. But the fuse is OK.

!image-20240812-061657.png|width=702,height=493,alt="image-20240812-061657.png"!

!1. After salt crystal PIN resistor value.mp4|width=720,height=1280,alt="1. After salt crystal PIN resistor value.mp4"!



!2. Returned to normal PIN resistor value 202K-8.2K-202K.mp4|width=720,height=1280,alt="2. Returned to normal PIN resistor value 202K-8.2K-202K.mp4"!

column24:
Jul/02/2024 2:26 AM;59f9c5b85556ec74b24dcc9b;Hi team, after discussing it with Hongbo.Lin, I think it was a matter of accidental contact. The alarm is LED1456, indicating that MCH has no power. First of all, it's not a software BUG, so far, we haven't had this kind of software BUG, this is just a general simple operation, if it is a software BUG, it should appear many times in many places. Secondly, it is not like a hardware problem, he has done ABA testing and disassembly investigation in the field, and then the problem has not been repeated.

The final judgment was accidental contact, it may have happened that the installation was not properly, such as the Pogo PIN twist or not fully stretch out. But when you remove-re-install it again, the problem is likely to be corrected unintentionally. Let's take a look at this picture, if it is the “problem accessory”, its PIN pin surface is uneven, and there is debris, easy to cause occasional poor contact.

!image-20240702-062609.png|width=367,height=262,alt="image-20240702-062609.png"!

 I suggest holding this JIRA for a while, and if there are no similar circumstances, shutting it down as a one-time event.

Sep/30/2024 9:36 AM;62f11c44da8620d5339425be;[~accountid:60c0bccf18e9f60071fa23d7] 

# For *Low* flow - Team used test SW that allows unit to change speed 50-60k RPM in 1k increments. finding that no magic number. current 50k setting will likely be best choice based on overall noise experience.
# For *Medium* flow - Team finding that 78k works best to eliminate piercing high frequency pitch (Blade pass frequency). 78k bring frequency to 19.5kHz based on motor having 15 blades (past threshold human hearing). Most adults can hear up to 17k Hz.
## Finding positive change for bare unit and all accessories except Straightener.
## Straightener seems to resonate a different sound being investigated by BOS acoustics team. Team may decide to keep current medium RPM setting for straightener only (70k).

Aug/11/2024 11:13 PM;712020:335537e7-4283-4cfa-a78f-973b0cd63d60;Team, 

* *Main Unit Spring Noise -* some units had a “squeaky” spring in the accessory latch on the unit which was audible and sounds cheap. We did not accept this for MP and the team is working on root cause. We believe this exacerbates the problem on the straightener.
* *Attach Click -* MB outlets were out of spec with a too large OD, leading to higher friction and worse click. We reviewed a MP trial mold sample with the correct OD and the click was great!  (Even on straighteners!!). This sample also fully resolved concentrator attach difficulty due to rotation.
** We need to assess a larger quantity of MP samples to verify an in-spec outlet OD in addition to the other optimizations we made enables a good click on 100% of units and straighteners.

I've discussed this with EE before and they think it's a process of normal software conditioning, which is really low risk.

* In the first 2s, the motor air fow has not been stabilized, and FW is adjusting the heater power ratio, so there may be run red.
* After 2s, after the air flow and NTC perception are stable, there will be no more redness, I spoke to EE and they believe this is normal software work and there is low risk

[~accountid:5fc5b70f0d2f61006f98e31c]  But please confirm whether the optimization software can solve it? I've learned that's not possible.

CC EE team[~accountid:5fc6eb47facfd6007634e0cf] [~accountid:61a81ea2b43d5b006a6fc742]

Jul/22/2024 3:24 AM;5fc5b70f0d2f61006f98e31c;[~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d]  Thanks for your focus, we defined as a risk based on below factors.

Aug/07/2024 3:06 AM;557058:dd41bfe2-9a5f-413f-b178-46f3dfdae569;[~accountid:5cbe1c2dcf9b780e6a39fd1e] [~accountid:6124bfff0952180069d65b9d] [~accountid:712020:c7ad2e4c-dc8b-44dc-997e-fec147725a95] [~accountid:712020:d87424d8-0784-4b92-aa1d-1b41a27c6c1e] [~accountid:5d546aa43f5e050c17177d02] below is the info I gathered from Hunk:

Per UL’s requirement, for this power input test, it would be classified into 2 categories:

# when there are active accessories, UL measures the power input with active accessories. If the power input doesn’t exceed the rated power, it would be considered as pass. We chose 1500W as the rated power because this is highest power when ARB is attached. 
# when there are passive accessories, UL only measures the power input of the main unit. As long as the power input is less than the rated power, it’s a pass.

On our DQTP, it says power input to be 1500W ± 10% which is ambiguous. It sounds to me that the power input needs to be 1500W ± 10% for everything. But this is not really achievable. For example, when the curler is attached, the motor RPM increases to 130K. The power input has to increase to do that. If we use UL’s standard, our EB1 units with ARB have passed the test. 

!image-20240807-070749.png|width=957,height=111,alt="image-20240807-070749.png"!

I think we should consider updating the DQTP criteria for this test.

column12:
A portion of the air flows through the hinge to cool the PCBA,
But the cool button/wind temperature/wind speed and the air duct are connected,
There will be airflow coming out of the button gap
column13:
Since it is not possible to plug all the gaps by design, we try to minimize the amount of air leakage

*Around Cool button*: 1.Reduce the clearance around the cold air button    2.add one Mylar sheet around Cool button.   3.The ribs behind the PCBA were removed to improve the air duct

*Around Air/Flow Button*:1.seal the gap between the lef/right inner handle.

*Power button*:1. add material to seal the gap between the handle housing and switch push rod. 2. add ribs on the switch push rod.

*Accessories Latch:* 1. change the EVA thickness from 1mm to 1.8mm for between seal between the left/right motor housing. 2. seal the hole for SW burn wire which is for EB verification.

post MP- add silicon gasket inside the handle swivel position to reduce more air to come into lower handle.(cost up $0.18,failed at DOE, not recommended for implementation)

column12: Insufficient baking temperature results in weak adhesion of some products, requirement:90±5℃, but the actual temperature in some areas is only 60~70 ° C
column13:
Updated SOP to define that the temperature of all ovens dedicated to logo pad printing is 90±5℃, and the baking time is 20min.

Update the IPQC checklist to add the check items for spot checking of the actual temperature of the drying passage using an infrared thermometer, with checking frequency of 2H/time

Change the pad printing supplier and import it through internal ECN (the test and verification of the new supplier has been completed, all the results are qualified, and batch import is expected to start on 4/14)

Update PFEMA to add risk control points where the actual temperature of the drying passage will affect the effect of the pad-printing product.

LED6 flashes to indicate a fault alarm after the air inlet is blocked. Preliminary analysis is that after a few cycles of ABR, the air inlet was blocked by dust, causing the temperature to exceed the threshold of low wind and high temperature when starting up, causing LED6 to flash.

The attachment is a simulation test of the bulk version and the current version, using a hand-held air inlet to simulate most clogging situations. Comparatively speaking, the new design is more sensitive, so EB1 version will be compared with the bulk version to optimize the software.

column4: Leshow HD400INTL series Outlet Grille and Heater, ECN20240107-012025_EB1_the unit on HH mode blow cold air and continued to running 10s unit stopped working with LED4&LED5&LED6 flashing during Thermal Stress test.
column12:
The whole test and inspection process requires a cold air to cool the PCBA, but the cold air was not directed at the PCBA during the inspection of the sample, which caused the PCBA to overheat.

In this situation, there is no wind blowing to cool down the PCBA, causing the Q1 overheating and it’s temperature exceed the upper limit of normal operation, so that it is always in the on-state. Thus the heater was always in heating condition, and finally caused the heater fuse broken .
column13: Inform lab about the precautions during inspection: the cold air should be aimed at the PCBA for effective heat dissipation
column14:
Mar/12/2024 9:22 AM;61a81ea2b43d5b006a6fc742;[~accountid:5d79159348eec30c3bca0848] [~accountid:61b4a597fe9f3000683df097] LED4&LED5&LED6 flashing indicate Heat NTC open/ short circuit.  After tear down this unit, the heater fuse was found broken.  The failure unit has been sent to GB for further analysis. Please refer to the  FA report from GB.  The root cause was that thyristor Q1 was always in the on-state. In this  situation, there is no wind blowing  to cool down  the  PCBA, causing the  Q1  overheating  and  it’s temperature  exceed the upper limit of normal operation, so that it is always in the on-state. Thus the heater  was always  in heating condition, and finally cause d the heater fuse broken .  

[^HD400UK-ST fault analysis of thermal stress test-20240312.pptx]
column15:
Mar/13/2024 9:58 AM;61b4a597fe9f3000683df097;Let me clarify the process.

This test takes the PCBA out of the inside of the unit and connects the unit with wires.

Put the outside PCBA in a 45 degree box for 100h, and the cold air cooled the PCBA. There was no abnormality in the whole test.

In the inspection unit, we still need cold air to cool the PCBA, but the inspectors did not aim the cold air at the PCBA, resulting in excessive temperature of the PCBA.

We have verified that there will be no abnormality if the cold air is directed at the PCBA during inspection, and if the cold air is lost, the PCBA will overheat protection.

!image-20240313-135736.png|width=1702,height=1276!

!image-20240313-135813.png|width=1702,height=1276!

!image-20240313-135748.png|width=1004,height=422!

column12:
Curler issue.

This issue appeared in the left curler of this batch. After replacing the left curler of other batches, this issue was not found. 

Abnormal curler check
The left curler C2 tooling air outlet has flash at position 1234
flash changed the direction of the wind, resulting in the inability to pick up hair at a distance of 1in

!image-20240229-120257.png|width=188,height=102!

!image-20240229-120302.png|width=188,height=89!
column13:
1.Removing the curler internal flash, the curler can pick up 1in distance from the hair.

2.Repair C2 tooling to eliminate flash

3.Strictly control the flash of plastic parts, not more than 0.05mm

!image-20240229-120701.png|width=246,height=217!

4.Each batch is checked for shipment - pick up hair 1in distance

Feb/04/2024 10:32 PM;61a81ea2b43d5b006a6fc742;[~accountid:61b4a597fe9f3000683df097] In this drop test, the damaged  FOC PCBA may have been caused by the tin residue (or other metal left over during assembly) left between the fuse F1 and the capacitor C5 during the drop test, resulting in a short circuit between the power supply live line and 15V. Please refer to the FA report from GB.  From the report ,  the fuse F1, rectifier bridge DB1, chip capacitor C5, power chip U16 and MCU  in the FOC PCBA were damaged.  The specific damage of the rectifier bridge need to be sent to the supplier for further analysis.   Will update later.  So the root cause of this ticket was not  C3 capacitor pin loose during drop test.

column12:
Software error protection

Remark: Restart to restore normal, the probability of occurrence is very low, and cannot be replicated in the same unit.
column13:
Short-term solution: EE analyzed and checked this sample, but could not reproduce the same phenomenon, and no abnormal signal appeared, confirming that this was a single case

Long-term solution: Cost down PCBA has a different protection logic and does not appear LED1,2,3 light flashes. Resolve this issue in Cost down PCBA ECN [ECN20231109-004233|https://quality.euro-pro.com/Lists/ECN%20Live/Item/displayifs.aspx?List=50868dd9%2D9eb9%2D4c90%2Db4bc%2D8201a0fdcc3a&ID=16910&ContentTypeId=0x0100102F2E983C283C4C8AFAD1024298D2FB]

HD400 Cost down PCBA uses a different protection logic and it will not happen same issue again.

column13: The action is cleaning shaft surface residual oil to add glue slots on shaft surface feature

Nov/23/2023 8:43 PM;60891091ccda29006b77ff85;[^SN Thermostat Function B test中发现电机不能启动 2023.11.22.docx]

This motor failed by the glue insufficient between magnet and shaft. It caused rotor magnet and shaft loosed and magnet broken ultimately. 

The action is cleaning shaft surface residual oil to add glue slots on shaft surface feature, this can ensure sufficient glue been adopted. The pull off force increased obviously. 

The improved motor needs to be verified in next EB.

HT200 Leshow dual-dource power cord supplier _ELE(ECN20230706-022357)

EB2_2/3 units(K02,K03) tested,L wire broken strands 39.02%, 58.54% more than spec 10%.

!image-20231024-093355.png|width=959,height=761!

!image-20231024-093417.png|width=891,height=698!
column12:
Present feed point is located on the bar with the most concentrated force and the largest bending angle. The surface of the feed point itself is uneven, easy to be stretched wider and thinner to crack after being stressed, the stress is transmitted to the copper wire and easy to break。

!image-20231031-063530.png|width=538,height=373!
column13:
The position of the feed point is adjusted from NO# 1 place where is most affected by stress to the NO# 2 place  where  is  less affected by stress.

Increasing the TCO shrapnel pressure value from 20-30g to 25-35g.

For this unit,  first stem we  checked the heat NTC with digital multimeter and found no problem. After, we did the cross validation: change a new heater, the unit can work well. !PCOPT-971.mp4|width=480,height=854!
column12:
it is in open-loop condition,  close the outlet step by step, *fuse broken at 9’7*” , *so it is only cool wind after 9’7''*

after block the outlet, the motor speed will increase rapidly due to it without loading (per the motor specification this load status is not allowed).

*when continue to run the unit, because no cool air to cool the thermal energy created by high-speed motor, several minutes later, the motor coil will burn out. that is why the unit stop to blow the cool air at 20’10*”

- CN team have checked and find the motor coil is burnt

column12: Software issue. The motor power limit protection at HF mode inside FW V03.01  was imperfect.
column13: To upgrade the FW version to the latest FW V03.03
column14:
Aug/09/2023 5:21 AM;61a81ea2b43d5b006a6fc742;[~accountid:624f8ec9f6a2690069620ea4] [~accountid:6166916772f6970069d12923] LED123 flash indicates the error of Power over limit

I received this unit from William today.   The problem can be reproduced @ 100V AC,  60Hz .   The unit function well @ 100V,50Hz.

After reflashing the  unit with FW V03.03, the unit  ran normally  without this problem  @ both 100V AC ,50Hz and 60Hz

The motor power limit protection at HF mode inside FW V03.01  was imperfect.    FW V03.03 has been optimized motor power limit protection at HF mode .

This problem can be solved by upgrading the FW version to the latest FW V03.03.  Please refer to the  software change history of FW V03.03.

!image-20230809-092018.png|width=1022,height=281!
